front: What is the Church-Turing Thesis and why is it significant?
back: The Church-Turing Thesis is a hypothesis stating that any function that is effectively computable by an algorithm can be computed by a Turing Machine. It is significant because it provides a robust and widely accepted definition of what 'computation' means, linking formal mathematical models to the intuitive notion of an algorithm.

front: What is the Halting Problem and what does its undecidability imply?
back: The Halting Problem is the problem of determining, from a description of an arbitrary computer program and its input, whether the program will finish running (halt) or continue to run forever. Its undecidability, proven by Alan Turing, implies that there is no general algorithm that can solve this problem for all possible program-input pairs, establishing a fundamental limit of computation.

front: What are the classes P and NP in computational complexity theory?
back: P is the class of decision problems that can be solved by a deterministic Turing Machine in polynomial time. NP is the class of decision problems for which a proposed solution (certificate) can be verified by a deterministic Turing Machine in polynomial time. The P vs NP problem asks whether every problem in NP is also in P, and is one of the most famous unsolved problems in computer science.

front: What does it mean for a problem to be NP-Complete?
back: A problem is NP-Complete if it is in NP and every problem in NP can be reduced to it in polynomial time. This means that if any NP-Complete problem has a polynomial-time solution, then P = NP. The Boolean Satisfiability Problem (SAT) was the first problem proven to be NP-Complete (Cook-Levin Theorem).

front: What is the role of reductions in complexity theory?
back: Reductions are a method for comparing the computational difficulty of different problems. A polynomial-time reduction from problem A to problem B transforms instances of A into instances of B, proving that if B is tractable (in P), then A is also tractable. Conversely, if A is intractable (e.g., NP-Hard), then B must also be intractable. Reductions are key to defining and proving NP-Completeness.
